quiz_questions.question_text: The P wave on an ECG represents:

options.text: Ventricular contraction
Valve closure
Atrial depolarization
Ventricular relaxation

quiz_questions.explanation: The P wave is the first positive deflection on a normal ECG tracing and represents the electrical depolarization wave spreading across both atria from the SA node, triggering atrial contraction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A normal PR interval is:

options.text: 0.12-0.20 seconds
0.30-0.40 seconds
0.50-0.60 seconds
0.04-0.08 seconds

quiz_questions.explanation: The PR interval (from the start of the P wave to the start of the QRS complex) normally ranges from 0.12 to 0.20 seconds, representing the time for atrial depolarization and conduction delay through the AV node.

quiz_questions.question_text: The QRS complex on an ECG represents:

options.text: Atrial depolarization
Atrial repolarization
Valve opening
Ventricular depolarization

quiz_questions.explanation: The QRS complex is the largest deflection on the ECG and represents the rapid depolarization of both ventricles; it is larger than the P wave because the ventricular muscle mass is much greater than that of the atria.

quiz_questions.question_text: The T wave on an ECG represents:

options.text: Ventricular depolarization
Atrial repolarization
Ventricular repolarization
Atrial depolarization

quiz_questions.explanation: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization, the process by which ventricular muscle cells recover their resting membrane potential after contraction, preparing them for the next depolarization cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: In normal sinus rhythm, each heartbeat shows:

options.text: QRS complexes without any T waves
Each P wave followed by a QRS complex, at a regular rate of 60-100 bpm
Irregular spacing between all waveforms
No P waves before QRS complexes

quiz_questions.explanation: Normal sinus rhythm is characterized by a regular rhythm originating from the SA node with each P wave followed by a QRS complex at a consistent PR interval, at a rate between 60-100 bpm, with normal QRS width and upright T waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: ST elevation on an ECG suggests:

options.text: Bradycardia
Acute myocardial injury (STEMI)
A normal finding in all patients
A healthy, well-conditioned heart

quiz_questions.explanation: ST-segment elevation indicates acute transmural myocardial injury from complete coronary artery occlusion; it is a medical emergency requiring immediate reperfusion therapy to salvage ischemic heart muscle.

quiz_questions.question_text: ST depression on an ECG may indicate:

options.text: Atrial fibrillation
A completely normal ECG
A normal variant seen in athletes
Myocardial ischemia (reduced blood flow without complete blockage)

quiz_questions.explanation: ST depression indicates subendocardial ischemia, where reduced coronary blood flow causes oxygen deprivation to the inner layers of the myocardium without complete vessel occlusion or transmural injury.

quiz_questions.question_text: Atrial fibrillation on an ECG shows:

options.text: Flat line with no activity
Wide QRS complexes only
No identifiable P waves with an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm
Regular P waves before each QRS

quiz_questions.explanation: In atrial fibrillation, the atria fire chaotically at 350-600 impulses per minute, producing a wavy, irregular baseline with no distinct P waves; the AV node conducts these impulses irregularly, creating the hallmark 'irregularly irregular' ventricular rhythm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A prolonged PR interval (>0.20 seconds) suggests:

options.text: First-degree AV block (delayed AV conduction)
Normal AV conduction
Atrial flutter
Ventricular tachycardia

quiz_questions.explanation: A PR interval exceeding 0.20 seconds indicates first-degree AV block, a conduction delay through the AV node in which every atrial impulse still reaches the ventricles but takes longer than normal; it is usually benign but may indicate underlying conduction disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sinus bradycardia on an ECG shows:

options.text: Normal P-QRS-T pattern but rate below 60 bpm
Absent P waves
Heart rate above 100 bpm
Wide QRS complexes

quiz_questions.explanation: Sinus bradycardia maintains all the normal waveform relationships (P wave before each QRS, normal PR interval, narrow QRS) but at a rate below 60 bpm; it can be physiological in athletes or pathological if symptomatic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the QT interval?

options.text: The time between atrial contraction and ventricular contraction
The duration of the P wave only
The time between two consecutive P waves
The total duration of ventricular depolarization and repolarization, from the start of the QRS to the end of the T wave

quiz_questions.explanation: The QT interval spans from the beginning of the QRS complex to the end of the T wave, representing the total time for ventricular depolarization and repolarization; a prolonged QT interval increases the risk of dangerous ventricular arrhythmias like torsades de pointes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 12-lead ECG?

options.text: An ECG that only measures heart rate
A 12-hour continuous heart rhythm monitor
An ECG that uses 12 separate machines
A standard ECG recording that provides 12 different views of the heart's electrical activity from various angles

quiz_questions.explanation: A 12-lead ECG uses 10 electrode placements to create 12 different electrical viewpoints of the heart (6 limb leads and 6 precordial leads), allowing clinicians to localize areas of ischemia, injury, or conduction abnormalities to specific regions of the myocardium.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sinus tachycardia' mean?

options.text: An abnormal rhythm not originating from the SA node
A rhythm with no P waves and a fast rate
A normal sinus rhythm with a rate exceeding 100 bpm, often a physiological response to exercise, fever, or stress
A rhythm with widened QRS complexes

quiz_questions.explanation: Sinus tachycardia is a regular rhythm with normal P-QRS-T morphology originating from the SA node at a rate above 100 bpm; it is usually a physiological response to increased demand (exercise, fever, pain, dehydration) rather than a primary cardiac problem.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ventricular tachycardia (VT)?

options.text: A slow rhythm with narrow QRS complexes
An irregular rhythm with normal P waves
A life-threatening arrhythmia with wide, regular QRS complexes at a fast rate, originating from the ventricles
A normal variation of sinus rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Ventricular tachycardia is a dangerous arrhythmia originating in the ventricles, characterized by wide QRS complexes (>0.12s) at a rapid rate (>100 bpm); it can compromise cardiac output and may degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ventricular fibrillation (VFib)?

options.text: A chaotic, disorganized ventricular rhythm with no identifiable waveforms and no effective cardiac output
ST elevation only without rhythm change
A slow but organized ventricular rhythm
A fast but regular sinus rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Ventricular fibrillation produces a completely chaotic ECG tracing with no recognizable P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves; the ventricles quiver ineffectively, producing zero cardiac output, and without immediate defibrillation, death occurs within minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'third-degree (complete) heart block' mean?

options.text: Complete dissociation between atrial and ventricular rhythms, with no impulses conducting from atria to ventricles through the AV node
A slight delay in AV conduction with all impulses eventually reaching the ventricles
An irregular rhythm with visible P waves
Occasional dropped QRS complexes

quiz_questions.explanation: In third-degree heart block, the AV node completely fails to conduct any atrial impulses to the ventricles, so the atria and ventricles beat independently; the P waves and QRS complexes have no relationship to each other, and the ventricles rely on a slow escape rhythm.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a wide QRS complex (>0.12 seconds) indicate?

options.text: A normal variant in young patients reflecting efficient rapid ventricular conduction through the bundle of His and Purkinje fibers
Atrial fibrillation with chaotic and disorganized electrical activity originating from multiple foci within the atrial myocardium
Normal, fast conduction through the ventricles via the His-Purkinje system, producing a narrow and efficient QRS complex pattern
Abnormal ventricular conduction, such as a bundle branch block or a rhythm originating from the ventricles

quiz_questions.explanation: A QRS complex wider than 0.12 seconds indicates that ventricular depolarization is not following the normal rapid conduction pathway (bundle branches and Purkinje fibers), suggesting either a bundle branch block or a ventricular origin of the rhythm.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the PR interval?

options.text: The time between two consecutive QRS complexes, representing the complete cardiac cycle duration and used to calculate instantaneous heart rate
The time from the onset of atrial depolarization (P wave) to the onset of ventricular depolarization (QRS), representing AV conduction time
The duration of the T wave, representing the time required for complete ventricular repolarization as potassium ions flow out of the myocardial cells
The time from ventricular depolarization to repolarization, representing the total duration of ventricular electrical activity during the cardiac cycle

quiz_questions.explanation: The PR interval measures the time from the beginning of atrial depolarization to the beginning of ventricular depolarization, encompassing atrial conduction and the physiological delay at the AV node; abnormalities in this interval indicate conduction problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'second-degree AV block Type I (Wenckebach)' mean?

options.text: Complete dissociation of P waves and QRS complexes
A conduction pattern where the PR interval progressively lengthens with each beat until one QRS is dropped, then the cycle repeats
Wide QRS complexes with a normal PR interval
A constant prolonged PR interval without any dropped beats

quiz_questions.explanation: Wenckebach (Type I second-degree AV block) shows a characteristic pattern of progressively increasing PR intervals until the AV node fails to conduct one impulse (dropped QRS); the cycle then resets with a shorter PR interval and begins again.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'systematic ECG interpretation' mean?

options.text: Examining only one lead of the 12-lead ECG
Looking only at heart rate to determine if the ECG is normal
Guessing the rhythm based on the patient's symptoms
A methodical approach that evaluates rate, rhythm, P waves, PR interval, QRS width, and ST/T changes in a consistent order

quiz_questions.explanation: Systematic ECG interpretation follows a structured checklist: assess rate, determine regularity, identify P waves, measure the PR interval, evaluate QRS width and morphology, and examine the ST segment and T waves to avoid overlooking important findings.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old man collapses at a gym. A bystander checks the AED and it advises 'shock.' After one shock, the monitor now shows a narrow-complex rhythm at 85 bpm with regular P waves before each QRS. What rhythm was he likely in before the shock, and what rhythm has been restored?

options.text: He was in atrial fibrillation; the shock converted it to atrial flutter
He was in ventricular fibrillation; the shock restored normal sinus rhythm
He was in normal sinus rhythm; the AED shocked unnecessarily
He was in sinus bradycardia; the shock sped up his heart to sinus tachycardia

quiz_questions.explanation: AEDs are programmed to analyze rhythms and only advise shocks for shockable rhythms (VFib or pulseless VT); the successful defibrillation terminated the chaotic ventricular fibrillation and allowed the SA node to resume its pacemaker role, restoring normal sinus rhythm at 85 bpm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nurse reviews an ECG that shows P waves occurring regularly at 80 bpm and QRS complexes occurring regularly at 35 bpm, but there is no consistent relationship between P waves and QRS complexes. What rhythm is this, and what treatment does it require?

options.text: First-degree AV block; no treatment needed
Normal sinus rhythm; the P waves and QRS complexes are just hard to match
Atrial fibrillation; requires blood thinners only
Third-degree (complete) heart block; likely requires a pacemaker

quiz_questions.explanation: Independent P waves and QRS complexes at different rates with no PR relationship is the hallmark of third-degree (complete) heart block; the ventricles are relying on a slow junctional or ventricular escape rhythm, and a pacemaker is typically required to maintain an adequate heart rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient presents to the ER with palpitations. The ECG shows no visible P waves and the R-R intervals are completely irregular with a ventricular rate of 140 bpm. The QRS complexes are narrow. What is the most likely rhythm?

options.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Ventricular tachycardia
Third-degree heart block
Sinus tachycardia with a fast rate

quiz_questions.explanation: The combination of absent P waves, irregularly irregular R-R intervals, and narrow QRS complexes at a rapid rate is pathognomonic (uniquely characteristic) of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, the most common sustained arrhythmia encountered in clinical practice.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is interpreting an ECG and notices the PR interval is 0.28 seconds (normal: 0.12-0.20s) but every P wave is followed by a QRS complex. What is the rhythm, and how clinically significant is it?

options.text: Third-degree heart block requiring emergency pacing
Normal sinus rhythm; 0.28 seconds is within the normal range
Second-degree AV block Type II requiring a pacemaker
First-degree AV block; usually benign but may indicate underlying conduction disease that should be monitored

quiz_questions.explanation: First-degree AV block shows a prolonged but constant PR interval (>0.20s) with every P wave followed by a QRS; it is typically benign and often an incidental finding, but can be seen with medications (beta-blockers, digoxin) or may indicate early AV conduction disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ECG shows wide, bizarre QRS complexes at a regular rate of 180 bpm with no discernible P waves. The patient is conscious but hypotensive with BP 80/50. What is the rhythm and the appropriate next step?

options.text: Sinus tachycardia; give IV fluids only
First-degree AV block; monitor only
Ventricular tachycardia with hemodynamic instability; prepare for synchronized cardioversion
Atrial fibrillation; administer anticoagulation

quiz_questions.explanation: Wide, regular QRS complexes at 180 bpm without P waves is ventricular tachycardia; because the patient is hemodynamically unstable (hypotensive), immediate synchronized cardioversion is indicated to convert the rhythm before it degenerates into VFib.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with chest pain has an ECG showing ST elevation in leads V1-V4. The cardiologist says this localizes the injury to a specific heart wall. Which wall of the heart is affected, and which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.text: The inferior wall; the right coronary artery is blocked
The lateral wall; the left circumflex artery is blocked
The anterior wall; the left anterior descending (LAD) artery is most likely occluded
The posterior wall; the posterior descending artery is blocked

quiz_questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads V1-V4 (the precordial leads overlying the anterior chest) localizes the infarction to the anterior wall of the left ventricle, which is supplied by the left anterior descending artery; LAD occlusion is often called the 'widow maker' due to the large area of myocardium at risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a routine pre-operative ECG, a 70-year-old patient is found to have a QTc interval of 520 ms (normal <460 ms for women, <450 ms for men). The anesthesiologist is concerned about this finding. Why is a prolonged QT interval dangerous?

options.text: It indicates the heart is beating too fast, with a ventricular rate exceeding 100 beats per minute that may require pharmacological rate control or cardioversion
It means the P wave is absent, indicating that the sinoatrial node has failed and an ectopic pacemaker in the AV node or ventricle has assumed control of the heart rhythm
Prolonged ventricular repolarization increases the vulnerability to dangerous arrhythmias like torsades de pointes, which can cause sudden cardiac death
It indicates first-degree AV block, where conduction through the AV node is delayed but every atrial impulse still reaches the ventricles with a prolonged PR interval

quiz_questions.explanation: A prolonged QT interval means ventricular repolarization takes abnormally long, creating a window of vulnerability during which a premature ventricular beat can trigger the lethal polymorphic ventricular tachycardia called torsades de pointes, potentially leading to VFib and sudden death.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient on a cardiac monitor shows a rhythm where every few beats the PR interval gets progressively longer, and then one QRS is missing entirely. After the dropped beat, the PR interval resets to a shorter length and the pattern repeats. A nursing student asks what is happening at the cellular level. What is occurring in the conduction system?

options.text: The AV node becomes progressively more fatigued with each impulse until it fails to conduct one, then recovers (Wenckebach phenomenon)
The ventricles are generating their own independent rhythm
The bundle branches are alternately blocking impulses
The SA node is intermittently failing to fire

quiz_questions.explanation: In Wenckebach (Type I second-degree AV block), the AV node's refractory period progressively lengthens with each conducted impulse (progressive PR prolongation) until one impulse arrives when the AV node is still refractory and fails to conduct (dropped QRS); the subsequent rest period allows the AV node to recover.

quiz_questions.question_text: A paramedic encounters an unconscious patient whose cardiac monitor shows a completely flat line with no electrical activity whatsoever. This rhythm is called asystole. Why does the AED not advise a shock for this rhythm?

options.text: Asystole always resolves spontaneously without any treatment
Defibrillation works by resetting chaotic electrical activity, but asystole has no electrical activity to reset; CPR and epinephrine are the appropriate treatments
The AED is malfunctioning and should always shock
Asystole can only be treated with medications, never with electricity

quiz_questions.explanation: Defibrillation depolarizes all cardiac cells simultaneously to allow an organized rhythm to resume, but this only works when there is disorganized electrical activity (VFib/pulseless VT); in asystole, there is no electrical activity to 'reset,' so the focus is on CPR to maintain circulation and epinephrine to stimulate cardiac electrical activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cardiology fellow is teaching medical students to interpret a 12-lead ECG. She shows an ECG with ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6, with reciprocal ST depression in leads III and aVF. She asks what region of the heart is affected. What is the correct answer?

options.text: Inferior wall MI affecting the right coronary artery
Posterior wall MI with no specific coronary artery involvement
Anterior wall MI affecting the LAD territory
Lateral wall MI, most likely affecting the left circumflex artery territory

quiz_questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 indicates a lateral wall STEMI, with the left circumflex artery being the most common culprit; reciprocal ST depression in the inferior leads (III, aVF) confirms the lateral localization and supports the diagnosis of an acute lateral MI.
